prior.title: California Energy Commission Business Meeting Materials and RPS Compliance Period 4 Summary
prior.summary: California Energy Commission publishes business meeting supporting materials and presentations from April-May 2026, including Summary Claims Details for Compliance Period 4 related to Renewable Portfolio Standard (RPS) Track requirements. Contact information provided through RPSTrack@energy.ca.gov.
prior.full_brief:
# California RPS Compliance Period 4 — CEC Verification and Business Meeting Status

## Market Signal Brief

---

# 1. What's Moving and What Triggered It

**Severity: MONITORING**

California's Renewables Portfolio Standard (RPS) Compliance Period 4 (CP4), covering reporting years 2021–2024, has now entered the formal CEC verification and adoption phase. This is the phase that converts four years of procurement claims into official compliance determinations — and it is moving now.

The California Energy Commission (CEC) has released three simultaneous deliverables that mark the opening of the CP4 verification window:

- **CP4 Summary Claims Reports** — now available.
- **Notice of Availability (NOA)** — issued and available.
- **25 Staff Draft Verification Results Reports** — now available for public comment.

*Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – RPS, CEC Programs Page. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard

CP4 covers the reporting years 2021–2024. *Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – Verification and Compliance, CEC. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard/renewables-portfolio-standard

The CP4 NOA and the 25 Staff Draft Verification Results Reports for POUs are listed and linked from the CEC's POU Verification page. *Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – Verification for Publicly Owned Utilities, CEC. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard/renewables-portfolio-standard/renewables

A parallel deliverable — the **RPS Verification and Compliance Methodology Report, Fourth Edition** — has also been released simultaneously with its own Notice of Availability. *Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – RPS, CEC Programs Page. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard

The trigger for this release is structural: CP4 ends with the 2024 reporting year, and California statute organizes RPS compliance on a multiyear compliance period basis rather than annual basis. The close of CP4 requires CEC to initiate formal verification before CPUC and CEC can issue compliance determinations to retail sellers and publicly owned utilities respectively.

The California Energy Commission conducts business meetings on a regular 2025 schedule, with meetings documented through December 2025, at which CP4 adoption items are expected to appear as agenda items. *Source: California Energy Commission, 2025 Business Meetings, CEC. https://www.energy.ca.gov/proceedings/business-meetings/2025-business-meetings

*Analytical inference:* The simultaneous release of the NOA, 25 Staff Draft Reports, and the Methodology Report Fourth Edition signals that CEC staff have completed internal verification analysis and the programme is entering its public-comment-and-adoption sequence, which — based on the CP3 pattern documented in the source materials — typically proceeds through staff draft, comment period, business meeting consideration, and final adoption.

---

# 2. Who's Driving It and What They Want

The CP4 process involves two California state agencies and four categories of load-serving entity (LSE), each with distinct roles and interests.

**California Energy Commission (CEC):**
The CEC verifies the eligibility of renewable energy procured by load-serving entities, which include retail sellers, publicly owned utilities (POUs), and all other entities serving retail sales of electricity in California that are obligated to participate and report to the RPS. *Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – Verification and Compliance, CEC. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard/renewables-portfolio-standard

For POUs specifically, the Energy Commission determines the Portfolio Content Category classification of procurement claims, calculates procurement requirements, and determines RPS compliance. *Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – Verification and Compliance, CEC. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard/renewables-portfolio-standard

The Energy Commission is authorized to issue violation notices for noncompliance and refer the POU to the California Air Resources Board to assess penalties. *Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – Verification and Compliance, CEC. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard/renewables-portfolio-standard

**California Public Utilities Commission (CPUC):**
The CPUC implements and administers RPS compliance rules for California's retail sellers of electricity, which include investor-owned utilities (IOUs), electric service providers (ESPs), and community choice aggregators (CCAs). *Source: California Public Utilities Commission, RPS Compliance and Reporting, CPUC. https://www.cpuc.ca.gov/industries-and-topics/electrical-energy/electric-power-procurement/rps/rps-compliance-rules-and-process/rps-compliance-and-reporting

After the California Energy Commission verifies RPS procurement claims for each compliance period, retail sellers submit Verified Compliance Reports from which the CPUC bases its compliance determination. *Source: California Public Utilities Commission, RPS Compliance and Reporting, CPUC. https://www.cpuc.ca.gov/industries-and-topics/electrical-energy/electric-power-procurement/rps/rps-compliance-rules-and-process/rps-compliance-and-reporting

**Four LSE Categories Subject to CP4 Verification:**
Load-serving entities (LSEs) include POUs, investor-owned utilities (IOUs), electricity service providers (ESPs), and community choice aggregators (CCAs). *Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – Verification and Compliance, CEC. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard/renewables-portfolio-standard

**The CP4 Target:**
California's load-serving entities are required to procure electricity from eligible renewable resources to meet 44 percent by the end of 2024. *Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – Verification and Compliance, CEC. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard/renewables-portfolio-standard

*Analytical inference:* The 44% procurement target for CP4 (end of 2024) is the headline compliance threshold against which all LSE claims in the 25 Staff Draft Reports are being measured. Whether individual LSEs meet, exceed, or fall short of this threshold is what the draft reports disclose — and those reports are now open for comment.

**WREGIS — Infrastructure Dependency:**
California's Renewables Portfolio Standard program relies on WREGIS to support program administration. WREGIS is an independent renewable energy certificate tracking system. *Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – RPS, CEC Programs Page. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard

WREGIS announced its agreement with its current software provider ends on December 31, 2027, and will not be extended. *Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – RPS, CEC Programs Page. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard

*Analytical inference:* The WREGIS software transition is a background risk factor running parallel to the CP4 adoption cycle. It does not affect CP4 determinations directly, but it is a forward operational risk for CP5 (2025–2027) and CP6 (2028–2030) tracking infrastructure.

---

# 3. Expected Trajectory and Conversion Triggers

**Phase sequence now underway:**

The CP4 verification process follows the same sequential structure documented for CP3 in the source materials:

1. **Staff Draft Reports issued** — the 25 Staff Draft Verification Results Reports for CP4 are now available. *Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – Verification for Publicly Owned Utilities, CEC. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard/renewables-portfolio-standard/renewables
2. **Notice of Availability and Request for Comments** — issued for CP4. *Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – Verification for Publicly Owned Utilities, CEC. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard/renewables-portfolio-standard/renewables
3. **Business meeting consideration** — CEC holds scheduled business meetings through the 2025 calendar year where CP4 adoption items appear. *Source: California Energy Commission, 2025 Business Meetings, CEC. https://www.energy.ca.gov/proceedings/business-meetings/2025-business-meetings
4. **Commission Final Reports adoption** — following business meeting vote.
5. **Compliance determination process** — after verification reports are adopted, the compliance determination begins according to the RPS POU regulations. *Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – Verification and Compliance, CEC. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard/renewables-portfolio-standard

*Specific date for CP4 final adoption not available from primary sources as of 2026-05-29.*

*Analytical inference:* Based on the CP3 pattern in the source materials — where the CP3 NOA and Staff Draft Reports were followed by a business meeting adoption cycle — the CP4 adoption sequence is likely to proceed through a CEC business meeting in the second half of 2025 or early 2026. The 2025 business meeting calendar runs through December 19, 2025. *Source: California Energy Commission, 2025 Business Meetings, CEC. https://www.energy.ca.gov/proceedings/business-meetings/2025-business-meetings

**Conversion triggers — when "draft" becomes "determination":**
- CEC Commissioners vote to adopt individual POU verification results reports at a business meeting.
- Once adopted, CPUC uses the CEC Verification Report to confirm each retail seller's annual REC claims and issues formal compliance determinations.
- For non-compliant POUs, the Energy Commission may issue violation notices and refer to CARB for penalty assessment.

**Compliance Period 5 has already opened:**
Compliance Period 5 covers 2025–2027. *Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – Verification and Compliance, CEC. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard/renewables-portfolio-standard

Annual reporting instructions for CP5 are already available, and the RPS reporting due date is July 1 every year. *Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – Verification and Compliance, CEC. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard/renewables-portfolio-standard

*Operational implication:* CP4 verification and CP5 annual reporting are running in parallel. LSEs simultaneously respond to CP4 draft verification comments while submitting their first CP5 annual report by July 1 of each year.

---

# 4. Operational and Cost Implications If It Materializes

**Re-check window: MONITORING — re-check at CEC business meeting adoption of CP4 Final Reports (expected 2025 H2 – 2026 Q1; exact date not yet published).**

**Penalty exposure — documented from prior periods:**
The CPUC's published compliance determination table documents penalty amounts for non-compliant retail sellers across CP1, CP2, and CP3. Examples from CP3 (2017–2020): EDF Industrial Power Services paid $460,185.00 for failing to meet the Portfolio Quality Requirement (PQR); Liberty Utilities paid $778,745.00 for the same. *Source: California Public Utilities Commission, RPS Compliance and Reporting, CPUC. https://www.cpuc.ca.gov/industries-and-topics/electrical-energy/electric-power-procurement/rps/rps-compliance-rules-and-process/rps-compliance-and-reporting

From CP2 (2014–2016): Gexa Energy paid $3,704,675.00 for failing to meet the long-term contracting requirement and PQR. *Source: California Public Utilities Commission, RPS Compliance and Reporting, CPUC. https://www.cpuc.ca.gov/industries-and-topics/electrical-energy/electric-power-procurement/rps/rps-compliance-rules-and-process/rps-compliance-and-reporting

*Specific CP4 penalty figures not available from primary sources as of 2026-05-29 — CP4 compliance determinations are pending adoption.*

*Analytical inference:* The penalty history across CP1–CP3 establishes the order of magnitude of financial exposure for non-compliant LSEs: from low hundreds of thousands to multi-million dollar figures, depending on the nature and scale of the shortfall. CP4's 44% target is higher than CP3's 33% target, and the LSE landscape now includes a larger share of CCAs alongside IOUs and ESPs, making the compliance picture more complex.

**Methodology Report Fourth Edition — new methodology implications:**
The Verification and Compliance Methodology Report, Fourth Edition is now available. *Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – RPS, CEC Programs Page. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard

*Analytical inference:* The release of a new edition of the Methodology Report concurrent with CP4 draft reports suggests that the analytical framework used to assess CP4 claims may have been updated. LSEs or their advisors reviewing CP4 draft results should compare the Fourth Edition methodology against prior editions to identify any changed eligibility determinations, Portfolio Content Category classification updates, or verification formula changes that affect their specific claims.

**WREGIS transition risk for CP5 and CP6:**
WREGIS's software provider agreement ends December 31, 2027 — which falls within Compliance Period 5 (2025–2027). *Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – RPS, CEC Programs Page. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard

*Operational implication:* Any LSE with CP5 procurement claims tracking through WREGIS faces a mid-period software transition risk. The CEC has directed stakeholders to follow WREGIS development updates directly. No continuity plan details are available from these source blocks as of 2026-05-29.

**For workspaces procuring renewable energy in California for Scope 2 or Scope 3 emissions accounting:**
*Operational implication:* RPS-certified RECs, tracked through WREGIS and verified by CEC, are the foundational instrument for renewable energy claims in California. CP4 verification outcomes — once adopted — will determine the official REC eligibility position of every facility whose generation a workspace may have relied on for emissions reporting claims covering 2021–2024. If a facility's RPS certification or generation claim is revised in the CP4 final reports, downstream REC-based claims in sustainability disclosures may require correction.

---

# 5. Competitive Implications

*No content for this section as of 2026-05-29: The source blocks do not contain sourced reporting on named competitor positions or named operator responses to CP4 that would allow competitive positioning analysis without invention.*

---

# 6. Client Conversation Talking Points

**What the workspace can credibly say:**

- California's RPS program has entered the formal verification phase for Compliance Period 4 (2021–2024). The CEC has issued 25 Staff Draft Verification Results Reports and opened a public comment period. This is the administrative step that precedes formal compliance determinations.
- The CP4 target was 44% renewable procurement by end of 2024. Whether individual utilities and CCAs met that target will be determined once the CEC adopts the final verification reports, which is still pending.
- Compliance determinations for retail sellers (IOUs, ESPs, CCAs) are made by the CPUC using the CEC's verification report as the authoritative input.
- Annual RPS reporting for Compliance Period 5 (2025–2027) continues in parallel, with a July 1 annual reporting deadline.

**Questions to pose to clients or counterparties:**

- Has your energy supplier confirmed their CP4 RPS compliance position under the CEC Staff Draft Reports? Has your REC-based procurement been verified as eligible?
- Are your renewable energy claims for 2021–2024 sustainability disclosures traceable to WREGIS-registered, CEC-certified facilities? If a facility's claim is revised in the CP4 final report, do you have a process to update your disclosures?
- Does your CP5 procurement strategy account for the WREGIS software transition risk by end of 2027?

**Claims to avoid:**

- Do not assert that any specific LSE is CP4 compliant or non-compliant — the final verification reports have not yet been adopted and compliance determinations are pending.
- Do not assert penalty figures for CP4 — no CP4 enforcement data is published as of the date of this brief.
- Do not assert that the Fourth Edition Methodology Report changes any specific eligibility outcome without reviewing that document directly.

---

# 7. What the Workspace Should Do Now

**Action 1 — Track the CP4 Business Meeting Adoption Calendar**
Monitor the CEC 2025 Business Meeting agenda releases (agendas typically available approximately 10 days before each meeting) for CP4 Final Verification Results Report adoption items. Subscribe to CEC Commission Business Meeting Agendas at: https://www.energy.ca.gov/proceedings/business-meetings/2025-business-meetings
- *Why now:* The adoption vote is the trigger for CPUC compliance determinations. Once adopted, enforcement timelines begin for non-compliant LSEs.
- *Re-check window:* Prior to each 2025 H2 CEC business meeting through December 19, 2025, and into 2026 if adoption does not conclude in 2025.

**Action 2 — Review the Methodology Report Fourth Edition**
Obtain and review the RPS Verification and Compliance Methodology Report, Fourth Edition, now available at the CEC RPS programme page. For workspaces whose renewable energy procurement claims (2021–2024) are tied to RPS-certified facilities, compare the Fourth Edition methodology against prior editions for any classification or eligibility changes affecting those claims.
*Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – RPS. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard

**Action 3 — File or Monitor CP4 Staff Draft Report Comments**
The CP4 NOA and Staff Draft Reports are open for public comment. Workspaces with direct LSE relationships, PPA counterparties, or REC-based reporting obligations tied to facilities in the 25 draft reports should review the relevant draft and determine whether to submit comments or respond to any findings that affect their supply chain.
- Comment filing: CEC's E-Filing and E-Commenting system (note: the system has been temporarily unavailable; CEC has stated that comment and filing deadlines will be extended while the system is unavailable and new deadlines will be posted). *Source: California Energy Commission, CEC Home Page. https://energy.ca.gov/about/divisions-and-offices/fuels-and-transportation-division

**Action 4 — Assess CP5 WREGIS Continuity Risk**
For workspaces procuring renewable energy or managing REC-based sustainability reporting in California through 2027: note that WREGIS's current software provider agreement ends December 31, 2027, coinciding with the close of CP5. Track WREGIS governance and software transition announcements at the WREGIS/WECC page referenced by CEC. Contact: rpstrack@energy.ca.gov.
*Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – RPS. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard

**Action 5 — Confirm CP5 Annual Reporting Obligations**
*Legal Confirmation Required:* whether a specific workspace entity — depending on its supply chain role (importer of electricity, buyer under a PPA, or REC purchaser without retail seller status) — has direct annual reporting obligations to CEC or CPUC under CP5 requires legal confirmation against the specific entity's California load-serving status and CPUC/CEC jurisdictional classification.

Retail sellers and POUs are required to report their RPS procurement claims annually to the Energy Commission on or before July 1 of each year. *Source: California Energy Commission, Renewables Portfolio Standard – Verification and Compliance, CEC. https://www.energy.ca.gov/programs-and-topics/programs/renewables-portfolio-standard/renewables-portfolio-standard

---
